drills.scenario: Priya is launching 'PawPantry', a Shopify pet-food brand. She owns nothing yet on Facebook — no domain, no Page, no ad account — and wants to run her first lead-gen campaign for a free feeding-guide.
drills.question: Put the foundational setup steps in the correct order, from a standing start to publishing the campaign.
options: Buy a new domain
Install WordPress / set up the site
Build the landing pages
Create a new Facebook Page
Create a new Business Manager account
Create a new ad account
Set up tracking (install/configure the pixel)
Create a lead magnet
Create and publish the campaign
drills.explanation: This is the author's exact foundational setup sequence (Ch. 2/3): domain -> site -> landing pages -> Page -> Business Manager -> ad account -> pixel -> lead magnet -> campaign. Tracking comes right after the ad account exists (an ad account is required before a pixel can live somewhere), and the lead magnet/campaign come last once the plumbing is in place.

drills.scenario: Marcus runs a local CrossFit gym. He's eager to launch and asks: 'Do I really need all this setup, or what's the bare minimum I must have in place before I can run a campaign at all?'
drills.question: What are the minimum prerequisites the module says must exist before running campaigns?
options: A completed WordPress site, a domain, and a lead magnet
A Facebook Page, an ad account, and your unique selling system
A Business Manager, a pixel, and an agency partner
A profile picture, a cover photo, and posted content
drills.explanation: Per Ch. 9, the minimum prerequisites to run campaigns are your Facebook Page (a business Page, not a personal profile), your ad account, and your unique selling system. The other items are part of the broader foundation but not the stated minimum.

drills.scenario: Elena, a business coach selling a webinar, is creating a brand-new ad account in Business Manager. She's based in Spain and bills in euros, but she's rushing and the form defaults to 'America/Los_Angeles' time zone and 'USD'. She thinks 'I'll just fix it later.'
drills.question: What's the correct call here?
options: Leave the defaults — time zone and currency can be edited in Business Settings anytime
Set the time zone and currency correctly NOW, because they cannot be changed after the account is created
Only currency matters; time zone auto-corrects to your billing country
Create the account first, then open a support ticket to switch both fields
drills.explanation: Pitfall from Ch. 9: you CANNOT change the time zone or currency once the ad account is created. They are permanent, so Elena must set Europe/Madrid and EUR correctly the first time. Getting this wrong would distort her reporting day-boundaries and billing.

drills.scenario: A new client tells you: 'I've been boosting posts from my own Facebook account where I have 4,000 friends. My ads keep getting weird and I can't access proper Ads Manager features.' You suspect he's been advertising from a personal Profile instead of a Page.
drills.question: What's the quickest reliable way to tell whether he's using a Profile or a Page, and what's the fix?
options: A Profile has 'Likes' and a Page has 'Friends' — tell him to keep using it
Profiles have 'Friends', Pages have 'Likes' — he's on a Profile and must create a proper business Page to run ads
Check whether it has a profile picture — only Pages can have one
There's no real difference; both can run ads identically
drills.explanation: Pitfall (Ch. 9): you must run ads from a Page, not a personal Profile. The quick tell is that Profiles have 'Friends' while Pages have 'Likes'. His 4,000 'friends' signal a Profile, so the fix is to create a business Page and run ads from there.

drills.scenario: Priya finished building the PawPantry Page but hasn't promoted it yet. Before she drives a single click of paid traffic, she wants to make sure the Page looks legitimate and 'open for business'.
drills.question: Which items should be completed on the Page BEFORE promoting it? (Select all that apply.)
options: Profile picture
Cover photo
Business description
A searchable username (not already taken)
At least some posted content
A minimum of 1,000 organic followers
drills.explanation: The Page-completeness checklist (Ch. 9) requires a profile picture, cover photo, business description, a searchable username, and some posted content before promoting — so the Page shows you're active and ready for business. A follower count threshold (1,000 followers) is NOT a requirement; promoting an empty/incomplete Page is the pitfall to avoid.

drills.scenario: Elena hires you as an outside consultant to run her webinar ads. She does NOT want to add you as an internal employee with a personal email seat — she wants to grant your agency access to her ad account using your agency's Business Manager.
drills.question: What do you need to give her, and where does she find it, to connect via the Partner method?
options: Your personal Facebook login; she finds it on your profile
Your agency's Business ID — the string of numbers after '?business_id=' in your Business Manager URL — used via 'Assign Partner'
Your Ad Account ID — the numbers after 'ACT=' in the ad account URL
Your Page username; she types it into 'Add New People'
drills.explanation: To grant an outside agency/consultant access (Ch. 9), the client uses your Business ID — found in the Business Manager URL after '?business_id='. She selects the Page or Ad Account -> Assign Partner -> 'Connect using your partner's business ID instead' -> inserts the ID. The 'ACT=' string is the Ad Account ID (used for adding an existing ad account, a different task), and 'Add New People' with an email is for internal team members, not partners.

drills.scenario: Marcus just created his gym's ad account inside Business Manager. He plans to start tracking 'months from now once campaigns are profitable' to avoid clutter. He asks if waiting on the pixel is fine.
drills.question: What's the best-practice guidance from the module?
options: Wait — install the pixel only after the first profitable campaign to keep data clean
Set up the pixel ASAP right after creating the ad account, so you start accumulating tracking, audience, and optimization data immediately
The pixel is optional for lead-gen; only ecommerce stores need it
Install the pixel only after you reach 1,000 Page likes
drills.explanation: Best practice (Ch. 25): set up your pixel ASAP after creating the ad account — a new ad account even prompts you to. Doing it early means you start accumulating tracking, audience, and optimization data as soon as possible. Waiting throws away data you can never recover, including audiences you could later retarget.
